direction: Hybrid Cyclotomic-GA Search for GS Difference Families over Z_167
rationale: The Goethals-Seidel construction is the most proven method for constructing Hadamard matrices of new orders (it produced H(1004), H(2524), H(1588) in recent work by Dokovic et al.). For v=167, the cyclotomic structure of GF(167) is simple (166 = 2x83) but can seed a genetic algorithm. The GA handles combinatorial explosion while cyclotomic seeding provides algebraic structure. This is the highest-probability path to success based on the literature.
first_step: Implement the Goethals-Seidel array checker in C++ with FFT-based autocorrelation evaluation. Generate initial candidate blocks from unions of cyclotomic cosets of order 2 in GF(167). Use these as seeds for a GPU-accelerated genetic algorithm with population size 50,000.
expected_signal: If a valid SDS exists for v=167 with cyclotomic structure, the GA should converge to fitness=0 within 10^6 generations. Convergence stalling at fitness > 0 indicates whether construction is almost possible (suggesting non-standard variant) or fundamentally blocked.

direction: Cocyclic Construction via Dihedral Groups D_{4x167}
rationale: Cocyclic Hadamard matrices over dihedral groups D_{4t} have been extensively studied and produced matrices where other methods fail. D_{668} has order 1336 and its H^2(D_{668}, Z_2) classifies all possible cocyclic Hadamard matrices. The Alvarez et al. GA for cocyclic matrices, combined with GAP cohomology computations, provides a systematic search framework. Recent results extend classification from order 36 to 44; scaling to 668 requires computational innovation.
first_step: Use GAP to compute H^2(D_{668}, Z_2). Enumerate representative cocycles. For each, construct 668x668 cocyclic matrix and evaluate orthogonality. Implement guided-reproduction GA to search within promising cohomology classes.
expected_signal: If |H^2(D_{668}, Z_2)| is manageable (< 10^6 classes), systematic enumeration is feasible. Otherwise, GA convergence rate indicates whether cocyclic H(668) exists in a particular class. Non-zero minimum defect across all classes would be a strong negative result.

direction: Parallel Tempering plus Kronecker Bootstrap from Paley(168)
rationale: The Paley Type I construction yields H(168) from the conference matrix of GF(167). While H(668) != H(4) tensor H(167), the structural proximity (668 = 4 x 167) suggests a Kronecker-product scaffold combined with physics-inspired optimization could succeed. Parallel tempering is the gold standard for frustrated systems, and starting from a structured initial condition should dramatically accelerate convergence.
first_step: Construct 168x168 Paley Hadamard. Build 668x668 scaffold by embedding four copies of the 167x167 core in a block structure mimicking the GS array. Run parallel tempering with 64 replicas and single-spin-flip dynamics for 72 hours on a 64-core machine.
expected_signal: Initial energy orders of magnitude lower than random start. Convergence to E=0 yields H(668). Convergence to small positive E (< 100) suggests the construction is close and targeted local repair could finish the job.

direction: Deep RL Agent for Sequential SDS Construction with Curriculum Transfer
rationale: RL has shown remarkable ability to discover non-obvious strategies in combinatorial games. The SDS construction has a natural sequential structure mapping well to an MDP. Curriculum learning (train on v=11, 23, 47, 59, 83 then transfer to v=167) can bootstrap the agent's strategy. The Cayley graph of Z_167 provides natural graph structure for a GNN-based policy.
first_step: Define MDP for SDS construction over Z_v with v=11 (order 44, known). Train GNN-based PPO agent. Evaluate success rate and compare with random search. Transfer to v=23, v=47. If effective, scale to v=167.
expected_signal: If RL achieves >50% success on v=11 and >10% on v=23, learned heuristics capture useful structure. Transfer to v=167: even partial solutions would be valuable as seeds for classical refinement methods.
